A blundering boyfriend from East Sussex, Jake Boys, surprised his girlfriend Emily-Victoria Canham with tickets to see One Direction and McBusted in Cardiff, but inadvertently booked flights to Dublin instead. The couple, both popular vloggers, are now planning to travel by train to the Welsh capital for the concert. One Direction will be supported by McBusted, a combined group of McFly and Busted members Tom Fletcher, Liam Payne, Louis Tomlinson, Dougie Poynter, and Harry Judd, and James Bourne and Matt Willis.

A helicopter crashed into a house in Sao Paulo, killing five people, including Thomaz Alckmin, the son of the city's governor, Geraldo Alckmin, and the helicopter's pilot, Carlos Isquerdo. Three mechanics were also reportedly killed during a test flight, and the cause of the crash is under investigation.

Andy Murray is set to marry Kim Sears next weekend after losing his seventh consecutive match to Novak Djokovic in the Miami Open final. Djokovic won 7-6, 4-6, 6-0, extending Murray's losing streak against the world No. 1 to ten out of eleven matches. The brutal heat of Florida took a toll on both players, with four breaks of serve in the first set coming from the same end due to the intense sun. Murray pushed Djokovic hard but ultimately couldn't keep up the pace, with the Serb taking the trophy and extending his record to 141 weeks atop the ATP rankings.

Bolton manager Neil Lennon is offering new contracts to veterans Emile Heskey (37) and Eidur Gudjohnsen (36), who joined on short-term deals in December and helped Bolton avoid relegation. Heskey scored one goal in nine Championship starts and was instrumental in Bolton's goalless draw against Liverpool in the FA Cup, while Gudjohnsen netted five times in the league and once in the cup. Goalkeeper Adam Bogdan (27) was also rewarded with a new deal, despite midfielders Barry Bannan and Neil Danns being fined two weeks wages each for a late drinking session that caused a disturbance at a hotel.

Arsenal's chief negotiator Dick Law has traveled to Argentina to finalize a £4.5million deal for 16-year-old Maxi Romero from Velez Sarsfield, but the transfer is being held up by ownership rights complications involving Romero's agents and his current club. Romero, compared to Lionel Messi, is expected to remain at Velez on loan for at least two more seasons before joining Arsenal.

A 21-year-old man named Alexander Pacteau from Glasgow's west end has appeared in court charged with the murder of nursing student Karen Buckley, and with attempting to defeat the ends of justice. Buckley, a first-year occupational therapy student at Glasgow Caledonian University, went missing following a night out at the Sanctuary nightclub and her body was later found on a farm six miles north of the city. Her family, who traveled from Ireland, are devastated by her death. Pacteau made no plea or declaration during the private court appearance and was remanded in custody.

A married teacher, Colin Turnbull, was caught having sex with a model, Rozina Khanim, in a classroom at a £13,000-a-year Catholic private school during an entrance exam day. Turnbull, 69, was found in a compromised position by the facilities manager, who informed senior staff. Turnbull admitted to improper behavior and was instructed to leave the school and never return. The school confirmed the incident involved two adults and no pupil was involved. Some sources expressed anger that Turnbull was allowed to leave without facing further consequences.

A study by Brown University found that frequent video game players have better visual learning abilities than non-gamers, as shown in a texture discrimination task. The researchers suggest that gamers may have a more efficient process for hardwiring visual task learning due to their extensive training. However, it remains unclear if gaming improves learning ability or if good learners are more likely to become gamers. A separate issue raised in the article is the concern of parents being reported to police for allowing their children to play violent video games, such as Call of Duty and Grand Theft Auto.

A nearly five-minute total lunar eclipse is occurring in western North America, starting at 3:16 a.m. Pacific Daylight Time and lasting until 4:58 a.m. Pacific Time. It is the shortest total lunar eclipse of the century, with parts of South America, India, China, and Russia also able to see the eclipse. The effect, known as the "blood moon," is caused by the Earth's atmosphere filtering out most of the blue light. NASA's Mitzi Adams will answer questions about the eclipse on Twitter.

Andy Carroll, the West Ham striker recovering from a torn left knee ligament, shared a photo on Instagram of himself posing on an oversized throne during the summer, with the caption "King Of Thrones!", despite continuing his rehabilitation at the training ground and missing the rest of the season due to injury. West Ham could benefit from Carroll's return, as they have only won one game in their last 11 and their European prospects have faded.

Former Liverpool striker Stan Collymore urges the club to show patience with under-fire manager Brendan Rodgers, arguing that his proven track record of promoting youth and developing talent make him the right man to lead the team back to success. Liverpool's disappointing season, which includes FA Cup and Europa League eliminations, has led some fans to call for Rodgers' dismissal, but Collymore believes he needs more time and resources to build a championship-winning team. Aston Villa's Fabian Delph scored the matchwinning goal in Liverpool's FA Cup semi-final defeat on Sunday.

Barcelona defender Adriano warned his team-mates to be on high alert against PSG's star-studded squad in the Champions League quarter-final, as even small mistakes could lead to goals from players like Zlatan Ibrahimovic, Thiago Silva, and Lucas Moura. Adriano singled out Lucas as a major threat, praising the Brazilian's skill and difficulty to cover.

IKEA's new virtual wedding service, Wedding Online, enables couples to get legally married and have guests attend from anywhere in the world through a group Skype-like platform. The service offers several ceremony settings and IKEA decorations for purchase, and allows for a six-hour live-streamed session with guests' webcams displayed on virtual bodies. Legally binding ceremonies require the couple, officiator, and two witnesses to be present in the same room.

The PFA Player of the Year award is set to be contested between Eden Hazard of Chelsea and Harry Kane of Tottenham Hotspur. Hazard is the favorite due to Chelsea's dominance in the Premier League, but Kane's prolific form for Tottenham, with 29 goals in all competitions, has earned him a nomination. Liverpool's Philippe Coutinho and Manchester United's David de Gea are also on the shortlist. Kane is also favored to win the Young Player of the Year award.

The Premier League football season is reaching its end, and the Player of the Year award is up for grabs as last year's winner Luis Suarez and 2013 victor Gareth Bale are no longer in the league. Six leading contenders include Harry Kane with 29 goals for Tottenham, Eden Hazard with 17 goals for Chelsea, Diego Costa with 18 goals for Chelsea, Sergio Aguero with 23 goals for Manchester City, David de Gea with 13 clean sheets for Manchester United, and Alexis Sanchez with 20 goals for Arsenal. Harry Kane, with his impressive goal-scoring record and work ethic, and Eden Hazard, who has been Chelsea's outstanding player, are strong contenders for the award.

West Brom manager Tony Pulis plans to hold talks with chairman Jeremy Peace after securing Premier League safety to discuss improvements and potential summer transfers, aiming to return West Brom to the top ten. Pulis identified Wolves' Bakary Sako as a target and expressed his belief that the club can once again become a top-ten team.

Astrophysicist Sean Raymond suggests that sun-less, wandering planets called rogues could harbor life due to their ability to retain heat from their interior or thick atmospheres, according to a study in Aeon. Rogue planets, such as PSO J318.5-22, without a host star, have been found to have masses similar to giant planets and could potentially maintain oceans.

The family of Martin Richard, who was killed in the Boston Marathon bombing, has asked for Dzhokhar Tsarnaev to be spared the death penalty in an op-ed, stating that continued pursuit of execution would bring years of appeals and prolong their pain. Tsarnaev was found guilty on all counts related to the attack and the penalty phase of the trial is set to begin next week, where the jury will decide between death or life in prison.

Reading manager Steve Clarke and his team will offer support to goalkeeper Adam Federici after his mistake led to Arsenal's winning goal in the FA Cup semi-final. Federici was visibly disappointed and attempted to prevent the ball from crossing the line after it rolled through his legs and out of his grasp, reminiscent of Robert Green's infamous error in the 2010 World Cup. Arsenal's Wojciech Szczesny comforted Federici as he left the field in tears.

Formula One drivers Lewis Hamilton and Jenson Button are using their time off from racing differently. While Hamilton enjoyed a jet surf session at home after winning the Bahrain Grand Prix, Button is training for the London Marathon with his wife and preparing with the help of ASICS. Button is running for Cancer Research UK and aims to beat his previous time of 2 hours 58 minutes.

A new study by the Australian Institute of Family Studies reveals that a majority of women who live alone in Sydney's Eastern suburbs, including successful professionals like Julie Sweet, a psychotherapist, and Lisa Hollinshead, a freelance publicist, hold university degrees and have healthy social lives, challenging the negative stigma that they are sad, lonely, and socially isolated. Despite this, many still face judgment and questioning from friends and family.

Ed Miliband's proposed change in the rules on non-domiciles, allowing foreigners and those with foreign links to pay tax only on money brought into Britain, could cost the Treasury significant sums instead of raising £1 billion as initially suggested, experts warned. A five-year grace period would exempt up to six in ten non-doms from the crackdown, as many do not stay longer than that.

Tanguy Pepiot, a University of Oregon runner, lost his lead and the race to Meron Simon of the University of Washington after celebrating prematurely with less than 100m to go, allowing Simon to overtake him and win by a tenth of a second. The crowd of over 3,000 people in Eugene witnessed the dramatic turn of events.

A biological father and his adult daughter, who are in an incestuous relationship and have two children together, have lost custody of their latest baby due to genetic health concerns and the father's criminal history. The pair, who were living together in Oregon, were sentenced to jail and probation for the incest charge.

A former Melbourne truck driver has revealed that some truckies in Victoria deal and use drugs while driving 60-tonne rigs, using secretive codes over their radio systems. One in every 12 truck drivers tested positive for drugs on Victoria's roads in the past year, and some are manipulating their log books to work up to 16 hours a day.

The WBC unveiled a $1million emerald green and diamond-encrusted belt for the Mayweather-Pacquiao fight in Mexico City, chosen by fans over an Onyx belt. Retired champion Larry Holmes and current light heavyweight Adonis Stevenson were present at the presentation, while Pacquiao continued training in Los Angeles.

An anonymous donor paid off the mortgage of Tim Ward, who survived last year's mudslide in Washington that killed his wife Brandy and left him grappling with the financial burden, after reading about his situation in a newspaper. Ward, who broke his pelvis in the disaster, was overwhelmed by the unexpected act of kindness.

Two Air Force nuclear missile launch officers, 1st Lt Michael Alonso and 1st Lt Lantz Balthazar, stationed at Malmstrom Air Force Base in Montana, face drug charges related to ecstasy, cocaine, and bath salts. Their cases will be reviewed at Article 32 hearings to determine if there is enough evidence for a court-martial. The charges come after a drug investigation uncovered illegal drug use among three launch officers and an exam-cheating scandal. Both officers are members of the 12th Missile Squadron, which operates 150 of the Air Force's 450 Minuteman 3 intercontinental ballistic missiles.

A family is seeking information on the whereabouts of Oliver Pareece Jones, a 37-year-old father of five from Colorado who went missing in Rancho Cucamonga, California, after making a $350 purchase at Walmart. Jones, who is described as a white male standing 5'8" tall and weighing 170lbs, was last seen in surveillance footage at the hotel and Walmart on April 5. His brother, Keith Jones, is pleading for any information on his whereabouts.

Mui Thomas, a 22-year-old woman with Harlequin ichthyosis, which leaves her skin red and raw, grew up in Hong Kong with adoptive parents who helped her manage her condition. She struggled with cyberbullying and denial during her teen years but now has a full-time job, plays sports, and speaks publicly about her experiences to inspire and educate others.

A teenage son of a Bulgarian housekeeper, who moved to the US as an eight-year-old and could not speak English, has been accepted into all eight Ivy League schools and ten other prestigious universities. The 18-year-old, Stefan Stoykov, is overwhelmed by the accomplishment and credits his hard work and parent's support for his success.

A mysterious cloud formation resembling the global Wi-Fi symbol was seen in Xiangtan city, China, leaving locals amused and perplexed. Student Shi Chao captured the image and shared it on social media, sparking jokes about the "giant Wifi Cloud" and its supposed incredible coverage. Previously, China has also reported UFO-shaped clouds and lenticular clouds.

A 19-year-old man from northwest London, identified as Yahya Rashid, was charged with terror offenses after being arrested at Luton airport upon returning from Turkey. He is accused of engaging in acts of terrorism and intending to assist others in committing such acts between November 1, 2021 and March 31, 2022. Rashid is due to appear in Westminster Magistrates' Court on Wednesday, according to London's Metropolitan Police, with CNN's Lindsay Isaac contributing to the report.

Orlando City in the Major League Soccer (MLS) has expressed interest in signing Manchester United's Javier Hernandez, who has struggled during his loan spell at Real Madrid. The Mexican international would be a commercial draw for Orlando due to their large Latin-American fanbase and the presence of Kaka. United teenage prospect Andreas Pereira is also in talks for a new contract, but European clubs like Paris St. Germain, Juventus, PSV Eindhoven, and Feyenoord are interested if he doesn't agree to terms at Manchester United.

A mother-of-three gained 12 stone and hid calorific treats in her medicine cabinet due to binge eating following post-natal depression and fears of her husband leaving her. She has since slimmed down to her pre-wedding weight after undergoing weight loss surgery following a health scare.

A North Carolina mechanic, John Brynarsky, discovered an inscription dedicated to his murdered son Chris, a custom paint job expert, on a repaired bumper in his body shop nine years after the crime. Chris's friend Mark Cosentino had written the message in memory of him before reattaching it to the car. The Brynarsky family was shocked but grateful for the memorial.

A rubber dinghy carrying around 100 African refugees across the Mediterranean sank, resulting in the deaths of 12 Christian passengers after a fight broke out between Nigerian Muslims and Christians when the latter began praying. The incident occurred amidst reports of an unprecedented 10,000 refugees fleeing war and persecution in Africa being rescued by Italian ships in the past week.

The Condemned Bali Nine pair, Andrew Chan and Myuran Sukumaran, have taken their clemency rejection challenge to Indonesia's constitutional court, intending to clarify the president's obligations when assessing clemency. The attorney-general has dismissed their move as a delay tactic, while the pair also aims to challenge the constitutionality of foreigners being barred from constitutional court appeals.

The article describes Teresa James, a 40-year-old woman who endures painful teeth whitening sessions despite the discomfort, believing the end result of a bright smile justifies the agony. Sales of tooth whitening products have seen a significant increase, with many women considering it an integral part of their beauty routine, leading to concerns about potential health risks from unregulated treatments.

The article discusses how liberal Democrats have found success in implementing their initiatives at the state and local level, despite gridlock in Congress. Examples include New York City's pre-K education program, New Orleans' housing program for veterans, and minimum wage increases in several states. The article encourages liberals to embrace local policymaking as a means of experimentation and preparation for future national legislation.

Former British Prime Minister Tony Blair, who is currently 61 years old, expressed his intention to continue in public life and advised governments around the world, planning to recruit other ex-leaders for the cause. He intends to remain active until he is 91 years old.

The article reveals insights from hotel industry insider Jacob Tomsky about common items guests take from hotels, including toiletries and towels, as well as less-known tips such as getting away with taking a hotel robe and the infrequent cleaning of minibar glasses and remotes. Tomsky also shares stories about the handling of unused toiletries and the discreet removal of guests who die in hotel rooms, and assures guests that their in-room safes are secure.

A UK concept artist, Alex Brady from Cambridge, has imagined a futuristic space station inspired by Las Vegas with neon lights and attractions for extravagant space weddings and parties, while also proposing single-stage-to-orbit vehicles for easier travel to orbit. His designs include efforts from Europe, the US, and India, and are a departure from the usual austere depictions of space stations. The artist's vision is not meant to be taken seriously but rather as a light-hearted exploration of a future where even a 'poor space miner' can enjoy zero-gravity roulette tables.

A student expelled from George Mason University for violating its sexual misconduct policy is suing in federal court to clear his name, arguing that an encounter with his girlfriend was consensual sadomasochistic role playing, not sexual assault. The university is demanding he identify himself publicly, but he argues doing so would expose not only him but the woman, as colleges face increased scrutiny for sexual misconduct responses.

Bereaved families of Britain's Iraq war dead have expressed their disappointment and frustration after learning that the long-awaited official report into the conflict may be delayed until next year, with some fearing a whitewash and accusing those criticized of trying to bury damning revelations.

An elderly couple, Charles Kingham, 86, and Pauline Moore, 68, have been living in darkness for four years after boarding up their windows due to persistent anti-social behavior and crime in their Mablethorpe home. The problems escalated when the house next door was converted into an integrated offender management center, leading to drug sales and frequent vandalism. Despite reporting incidents to the police, the couple has suffered health problems and feels trapped, unable to sell their £100,000 home due to the disclosable anti-social behavior history.

Thomas Bjorn faced a challenging second hole at the Masters after driving into the trees and needing a free drop when his shot landed in a spectator's lap. Russell Henley shot 68, while Ian Poulter was disappointed with his performance around the greens. Bjorn, Henley, and Bubba Watson all made par or better, while 35-year-old Erik Compton, making his debut, shot 73. Tom Watson chipped in for a surprising birdie on the 10th hole.

A snowstorm engulfed Krasnoyarsk, Russia's third largest city, in a matter of seconds, as seen in a video that captured the moment the Yenisei River was plunged into darkness. The storm, which moved at 45mph, instantly dwarfed the Kommunalnyi Bridge and reduced visibility to zero, with large grey clouds obscuring the sun. Reports suggest that the cyclone was caused by warm weather.

Juventus director Pavel Nedved has revealed that Barcelona are interested in signing Paul Pogba, but Nedved insists the French midfielder will not be leaving Juventus. Nedved expressed his relief that Juventus avoided Barcelona in the Champions League quarter-finals and praised the form of Lionel Messi, who he believes is currently in the best form of his career.
A fire destroyed a building in Flint, Michigan, which was formerly a strip club named The Body Shop. The cause of the blaze is currently under investigation.

Erica Ginnetti, a Pennsylvania math teacher, was sentenced to 30 days in jail, house arrest, probation, and must register as a sex offender for having sex with a 17-year-old student and sending him explicit videos. Judge Garrett Page chastised her for her "bad act" but does not consider her a "bad human being."

In eastern Russia, several baby bears have been rescued after their mothers were illegally hunted or abandoned, including Mitya, a camera-shy cub found outside a circus, and three siblings in Omsk. Elsewhere, Masha the bear cub was flown from Siberia to a sanctuary in Moscow after being found as a newborn, while a baby brown bear was rescued from being kept as a pet in the Ural Mountains. All of these cubs are now receiving care and are believed to be in stable condition.

Tony Pulis returned to Selhurst Park for the first time since leaving Crystal Palace and masterminded a 2-0 victory for West Bromwich Albion, pulling them to the edge of safety. Pulis, who saved Palace from relegation last season, was applauded by fans despite the timing of his departure. An early goal from James Morrison and a stunning strike from Craig Gardner secured the win, leaving West Brom with a healthy gap from the bottom three.

A 73-year-old councillor named Joseph O'Riordan stabbed his wife of ten years, Amanda, eight times with a seven-inch kitchen knife after discovering her affair with a postman, Nicholas Gunn. The attack occurred at their home in East Sussex, leaving Amanda with life-threatening injuries including wounds to her chest, torso, and arms. O'Riordan, who was also the president of his local Royal British Legion and had attended the Queen's garden party in 2013, pleaded guilty to wounding with intent but denied a charge of attempted murder.

Concerns have been raised over a 17-week bridging diploma offered by the University of Sydney to Year 11 students at Scot's College, which allows them direct entry to undergraduate courses without completing their final year certificate. The program, devised by the university's commercial arm, has stirred fears among student groups that it allows parents to buy their sons' university entry, with eight students gaining entry in 2014, while 166 students completed the HSC.

A traumatised mother, Lorraine Cobourn, developed a phobia of the color yellow after being hit by a yellow taxi, resulting in a broken hip and the loss of her job. She now cannot handle bananas or look at yellow number plates, and experiences anxiety when she encounters yellow taxis or sees the color in her peripheral vision. The driver of the taxi was later charged with driving without due care and attention and was fined for the incident. Ms. Cobourn is undergoing counseling and has received interim payments to help with her mortgage and bills while her injury lawyers deal with her compensation claim.

A driver in Gloucestershire lost the roof of his lorry, which was left suspended between a hedge and a tree after he blindly followed his sat nav down an unregistered country road. Police shared a picture of the mishap on social media with a warning for other drivers to be aware of the height of their vehicles and not always trust their sat navs on country roads.

The owner of the Egyptian-themed Florida mansion known as Pharaoh's Palace, advertised online as a private location for parties and a school for strippers, has been found in violation of county zoning codes and has decided to sell the property after repeated noise complaints from neighbors. Websites indicate that Pharaoh's Daughters, a school for strippers, had been operating on the property.

The article describes a guided hike along the historic Chilkoot Trail in British Columbia, where the remains of the Klondike Gold Rush are preserved. The hiker, accompanied by a First Nation guide, encounters relics of the past, including a leather boot and harness covered in mould, while taking in the breathtaking wilderness and reflecting on the epic journey of the gold rush prospectors, whose footsteps they follow on hallowed ground marked by lichen and other signs of time.

A video titled "Extra Storage Space" featuring new parents sharing their tips on surviving the first months with a new baby has gone viral, offering advice on everything from making room for date nights and taking plenty of photos, to being grateful for the experience and not trying to mold your child. The video, which has been viewed almost 600,000 times, also encourages viewers to submit their own parenting advice using the hashtag #makeroomforlife.

A woman named Nicole Mayhew, who had a strange feeling she needed to see her husband Scott, returned home to find him trapped and seriously injured under their SUV in the garage. After an hour and a half of waiting for help, Scott called out to his wife. Nicole called 911, and with the assistance of a neighbor, they managed to lift the car off him. Scott, who suffered six broken ribs, is expected to make a full recovery, and Nicole believes it was divine intervention that caused her to come home in time to save her husband's life.

Terrorist group ISIS has released photographs of its militants destroying Christian graves in Mosul, Iraq, using sledgehammers and justifying the mass desecration with a statement that suggests those buried above ground are closer to Allah than the living. The group has also destroyed historical sites, including Nimrud, and released videos of the destruction on social media. Many Muslims reject the justification, which stems from a collection of Islamic teachings known as the hadith.

Northern Ireland's Ards Borough Council spent over £7,000 on a taxpayer-funded Christmas banquet and disco for its staff before merging with a neighboring authority, with catering, decorations, and entertainment costing thousands. The three-course meal featured roast turkey and potatoes cooked in duck fat.

A Sydney couple, Epiphany Morgan and Carl Mason, traveled to 35 countries and produced one mini documentary per day for a year, despite initial doubts and a strict budget of $40 a day. They faced various challenges, including limited internet access and the need to engage with people, ultimately learning valuable lessons about human connection and compassion.

At her daughter's funeral, Lisa Colagrossi's mother accused her late daughter's boss, Camille Edwards, of overworking her, leading to her sudden death from a brain aneurysm at age 49. The confrontation took place at the church and was witnessed by mourners.

Eleven-year-old Charlie Patino, a talented midfielder discovered by Luton Town scouts, is weighing offers from Arsenal, Chelsea, and Tottenham, following in the footsteps of Arsenal star Jack Wilshere, who was also a Luton academy player. His parents are considering the offers while ensuring he continues to develop at Luton, with support from the club's CEO.

Carlisle United manager Keith Curle criticized his team's performance and some players' lack of professionalism after a 3-1 defeat against Accrington Stanley, which left them just above the League Two relegation zone. Curle expressed concern about the team's weak character, accountability, and pride, and considered organizing an open forum for fans to express their frustrations directly to the players.

Fashion designer Max Azria's 30,000 sq ft Los Angeles mansion, located in Holmby Hills and featuring a Moroccan-style bathhouse with a personal sauna and 22 bathrooms, is on the market for $85million. The estate, which includes a two-story-tall crystal chandelier, a 6,000 sq ft movie theater, and five distinctly-designed gardens, underwent a $30million renovation by Azria and his wife Lubov.

A man who was in the car with Walter Scott before his fatal shooting by Officer Michael Slager has spoken out, expressing regret and expressing that Scott did not deserve to die. Meanwhile, audio obtained by The Guardian reveals Slager laughing and admitting to feeling a rush of adrenaline after the shooting, which occurred following a traffic stop for a broken brake light. Slager, 33, is now facing murder charges and national scrutiny.

A tiger at the Hengdaohezi Siberian Tiger Park in China had a comedic mishap while attempting to catch a chicken in mid-air. The bird flew upwards unexpectedly, causing the tiger to slip on the icy surface and crash to the ground, leaving the chicken out of reach.

Following the controversial ending to the Chinese Grand Prix, where Lewis Hamilton was accused of slowing down to prevent Nico Rosberg from overtaking him, the Mercedes drivers had a heated disagreement in the aftermath. Both drivers publicly expressed their opinions, with Hamilton defending his actions and Rosberg accusing him of being a 'non-racer'. After meeting with team management, Hamilton declared the talks 'positive' and cleared both drivers of wrongdoing, while Rosberg offered his version of events in a post-race video.

English rugby league player Sam Tomkins has returned to his hometown club Wigan after cutting short his contract with New Zealand Warriors. He was sought after by both Wigan and Warrington but chose to reunite with his brothers and signed a four-year deal, with Wigan paying over £200,000 in transfer fees. Tomkins, who previously played for Wigan from 2008 to 2013, made 129 appearances and scored 107 tries. His departure from New Zealand Warriors signals the end of Matt Bowen's tenure with the club.

A teenager, Kazi Islam, is on trial for grooming and bullying his friend with learning difficulties, Harry Thomas, into helping him make a pipe bomb by sending coded text messages. Islam used the codeword 'cake' for 'bomb,' but Thomas failed to understand and exposed the plot. Islam also encouraged Thomas to buy parts for the bomb and to murder a soldier, and he tried to radicalize others at the college. The pair met in October 2013, and Islam began manipulating and exploiting Thomas' vulnerability.

An Asiana Airlines Airbus A320 overran the runway during landing at Hiroshima Airport in Japan on Tuesday, injuring 23 people and causing minor damage to the plane. The airline reported that there were 81 people aboard when the flight took off from Incheon International Airport in South Korea. The cause of the incident is under investigation.

CNN reports that Al-Shabaab, a Somali nationalist, Islamist group affiliated with al Qaeda, has carried out five major attacks in Kenya in the past year and a half, with the most recent one at Garissa University College resulting in 147 deaths. The group justifies its attacks as retaliation against Kenya's military presence in Somalia, but the weakness of Al-Shabaab, which has lost ground in Somalia due to African Union and US military efforts, is evident in its targeting of soft targets rather than military bases.

An African-American woman, Farryn Johnson, who was fired from a Maryland Hooters in 2013 after getting blonde highlights, is set to receive $11,886.40 in damages and $244,000 in legal fees following an arbitration ruling that determined she was illegally terminated due to racial discrimination. Hooters denies the claims and maintains that Johnson's termination was not discriminatory.

An al-Qaeda commander, Khalid Saeed Batarfi, was released from prison by terrorists in Yemen and celebrated by posing with an AK-47 inside the governor's palace in Mukalla, where he was joined by over 300 other freed inmates. The militants stormed the prison and overran government buildings and the city's port after rebels attacked the president's palace in Sana'a, leaving it unclear what resistance remains.

A study published in the journal Conservation Letters warns that up to 21 million acres of forests in south east Asia will be destroyed over the next decade to make way for rubber plantations, primarily for vehicle and aeroplane tyres, putting endangered species like gibbons, leopards, and elephants, as well as bat, deer, and beetle species at risk. Devastating impacts have already been seen on Indonesia's Borneo island, with many protected areas, including Snoul Wildlife Sanctuary, cleared since 2009.

A 20-year-old Indian cricketer, Ankit Keshri, died after suffering a cardiac arrest following a collision with a teammate while attempting to take a catch during a club match in Kolkata. India cricket legend Sachin Tendulkar and other Indian stars offered their condolences on social media. Keshri, who was considered a promising player, had captained the Bengal Under-19 team and was short-listed for India's under-19 team for the 2014 Colts World Cup. His death comes five months after the passing of Australian cricketer Phillip Hughes, who was hit on the neck by a bouncer.

In an unprecedented turn of events, England women's Under 19 team was given a second chance to retake a controversial penalty against Norway after a referee's error in the previous match. Leah Williamson, the team captain, calmly converted the penalty, securing a crucial 2-2 draw and qualification for the finals in Israel.

A new study published in the journal Cell Reports suggests that modern society's obsession with hygiene and sterile food has led to a decrease in bacterial diversity in the gut, making people more susceptible to allergies and overreactions to harmless allergens such as pollen. The research, which compared the bacteria in the guts of people from the US and Papua New Guinea, found that Americans had significantly fewer bacterial types than Papua New Guineans, bolstering the hygiene hypothesis.

A Briton, Phil Blackwood, sentenced to two-and-a-half years with hard labor in Burma for posting a mocked-up image of Buddha wearing DJ headphones on Facebook, has been "abandoned" by the Foreign Office according to human rights campaigners, despite being a British citizen. The Foreign Office claims they have provided advice and support to New Zealand diplomats leading the case.

Andy Murray, fresh from his wedding, enjoyed a second Barcelona victory in a week as he watched Lionel Messi and the team defeat PSG 2-0 in the Champions League quarter-finals, with best man Ross Hutchins by his side. Neymar scored both goals for Barcelona, securing their place in the semi-finals.

Sophie English, an Australian woman born during the Vietnam War and adopted at 10 months old, travelled to Vietnam with ABC's Foreign Correspondent reporter Sally Sara in search of answers about her birth mother. She met another adoptee, Le My Huong, who had found hers, and the emotional reunion provided valuable closure for English.

On Tuesday, a team of 34 Indian doctors from the India Institute, Ram Manohar Lohia Hospital, Safdarjung Hospital, and All India Institute of Medical Sciences, arrived at Kathmandu's airport to collect relief supplies from Pakistan. However, they discovered packets of 'beef masala' among the aid, which is considered offensive in the majority-Hindu country due to the sacred status of cows. The team chose to have food from a hotel instead and informed the Nepalese government, potentially triggering diplomatic acrimony between the South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation (Saarc) member countries. The Pakistani Army dispatched the relief goods, but could not be contacted for comment.

A father who took his 13-year-old runaway daughter to a radical extremist rally, where she may have been influenced by hate clerics and violent protests, later denied any exposure to extremism when testifying before parliament about her joining IS. The father, Abase Hussen, was caught on video at a flag-burning protest in 2012 and attended another rally with his daughter and wife.

A Baltimore community activist, Joseph Kent, was released from jail after being arrested during a live CNN broadcast for allegedly violating the city's curfew during protests over Freddie Gray's death. Kent's attorney confirmed his release and stated that Kent was trying to calm down the situation and prevent further violence when he was arrested.

A 18-year-old woman, Ann Malsbury, avoided jail time despite attempting to strangle her ex-boyfriend, Lee Judson, with an iPod cable and battering him with a saucepan and hair straighteners at her Coventry home. The incident occurred when Judson came to collect his belongings after their relationship ended. Malsbury, who was suffering from depression, jumped on Judson's back and wrapped the iPod cable around his neck, trying to throttle him. She later armed herself with a large carving knife and stabbed at him several times before calling the police herself. Malsbury was cleared of attempting to inflict grievous bodily harm and was instead given a community order with supervision and a women’s emotional wellbeing course.

A Pakistani supermodel, Ayyan Ali, was arrested and jailed in Rawalpindi after carrying over $500,000 in cash through Islamabad airport, exceeding the legal limit. The incident has caused controversy and sparked discussions in the country's fashion world, with some suggesting she was targeted due to her celebrity status. (The Guardian)

Human remains have been found in the search for missing college freshman Anjelica 'AJ' Hadsell, 18, who vanished while on spring break at home in Norfolk, Virginia, last month. The FBI is now involved in the investigation after the remains were found outside a residence in Franklin, around 40 miles from Norfolk. Hadsell's stepfather, Wesley Hadsell, was arrested for breaking into a home in connection to the investigation, but he insists he was looking for AJ. The cause of death and identification of the remains are pending at the Chief Medical Examiner's Office.

MI5, the UK's domestic intelligence agency, welcomed three young winners to its London headquarters for a special 'spy school' experience, as part of a competition run by children's TV show Blue Peter. The children, aged 10 to 13, were chosen from over 5,000 applicants after a series of tests and impressed with their observational skills, code-cracking abilities, and love for intelligence work. The visit came at a time when MI5 is grappling with major threats from terrorism and intelligence leaks, and the agency is also making efforts to recruit a more diverse workforce.

A new mother, Charlotte Bevan, and her baby were found dead near the Clifton suspension bridge in Bristol after she stopped taking an anti-psychotic medication, risperidone, prior to giving birth. Police believe her mental health issues may have contributed to her leaving the hospital without shoes, a coat, or her baby, resulting in their deaths on a cliff face. The hospital is conducting a review into the care they provided and the coroner is exploring the impact of the breastfeeding advice given regarding the medication.

Four QPR fans, including Harry Childs, have been jailed for affray and given Football Banning Orders after engaging in violent disorder outside a pub with Burnley fans following a 2014 Championship match at Loftus Road, resulting in numerous assaults and a shattered pub window.

St Mirren captain Steven Thompson apologized for accidentally spearing teammate John McGinn with a training pole, leaving the Scotland Under-21 player on crutches and out for the rest of the season. The incident occurred during a dribbling drill and Thompson, who scored two goals in the team's 4-1 win over Kilmarnock, has been devastated by the accident.

Disgraced former Tower Hamlets Mayor Lutfur Rahman, who was found guilty of ballot fraud and bribery, has been accused of playing the race card to silence critics by his deputy Oliur Rahman. The latter claimed there is deep-rooted racism in Tower Hamlets despite the legal ruling against his predecessor.

Brad Pitt and Angelina Jolie's Miraval Rose wine from their French vineyard, Chateau Miraval, has been sold in a British supermarket for the first time after receiving positive reviews from wine critics. The £18-a-bottle rose, described as having summer berry and wild herb flavors with a refreshing acidity, is now available at Marks and Spencer.

A Hungarian-born man, Szilveszter, now living in Bolton, regrets injecting petroleum jelly into his penis in an attempt to make it bigger, leaving him unable to have sex and in constant pain. After seeing no improvement with doctors, he turned to cosmetic surgeon Dr. Vik Vijh on TLC's Extreme Beauty Disasters, who successfully removed the Vaseline and scar tissue in a risky operation.

A goat causing a commotion in Paramus, New Jersey, was apprehended by police after it headbutted a door in a wealthy community. The animal was unharmed and handed over to animal control as officers search for its owner. Some speculated the goat may have been trying to avoid being someone's Easter dinner.

A Brisbane clothing company, Living Dead Clothing, has experienced unprecedented success after securing the licence to produce an exclusive line of Avengers: Age of Ultron merchandise. The demand has been so high that the website crashed, and the small operation has grown from a team of two to 15, with sales estimated to be worth tens of millions of dollars.

The article discusses the release of "Avengers: Age of Ultron" and its mixed reception, with some criticism of the action scenes and character development, but overall praise for the film's ability to deliver on the expectations of its massive fanbase and set the stage for future installments in the Marvel Cinematic Universe.

Sixteen-year-old Mick Schumacher, son of seven-time Formula One champion Michael Schumacher, makes his Formula 4 racing debut this weekend in Germany. Known for his intelligence and talent, Schumacher Jr. drives for Van Amersfoort Racing and starts in 19th position. Despite the pressure of following in his father's footsteps, he is expected to have a successful career in the sport.

Steve Bruce, Hull manager, was disappointed after unflattering paparazzi pictures of him on a Barbados beach were published, promising his wife he would go on a diet. He also expressed his support for FA chairman Greg Dyke's proposals to increase the number of homegrown players in Premier League squads and criticized clubs like Manchester United for not doing enough to produce young English talent.

A family of ducks made an unexpected visit to an office in Sutton Coldfield after employees helped them cross a busy road, with the mother and her ten ducklings staying for an hour and wandering through the corridors before being released back into a nearby park.

Tyra Banks made her awards ceremony hosting debut at the Daytime Emmy Awards in California, delivering on her promise of multiple outfits and a new short hairdo. Matt Lauer was featured in a prank bit where his image was substituted with a man wearing pasties and stockings, with the promise of a charity donation for each mention of his name in acceptance speeches. Winners included Laura Wright, Billy Miller, Steve Harvey, and Anthony Geary, among others.

Valencia moved within a point of La Liga champions Atletico Madrid in the fight for third place after a 3-0 derby win over Levante, with Sofiane Feghouli scoring the second goal. Valencia, who missed out on European competition last season, are now fourth and just one point behind Atletico, while Levante remain third from bottom on 28 points and are flirting with relegation. Feghouli told Spanish television that Valencia controlled the game against Levante and are focused on making things difficult for leaders Barcelona in their next match.

Former Real Madrid player and assistant coach Fernando Hierro believes the current Real Madrid team, who are set to face Atletico Madrid in the Champions League quarter-finals, have the capability to win the tournament for a second consecutive year, drawing on the club's strong and confident DNA and the successful European runs of past teams. Hierro, who won the Champions League three times as a player at Real Madrid, is confident in the team's ability to break the record of no back-to-back wins and emulate the successes of previous Real Madrid sides.

Scottish football clubs Hibs, Hearts, and Rangers have expressed their disappointment and concern over the SPFL's decision to move Rangers' final Championship game against Hearts back 24 hours, which could potentially give Rangers an advantage in the race for second place. Hibs and Hearts have lodged formal complaints with the league, arguing that all final round fixtures should be played at the same time to maintain sporting integrity.

A study reveals that a significant number of older workers, particularly those over 55, believe their outdated technology skills put them at risk for redundancy and long-term unemployment. This generation, which prides itself on literacy and numeracy skills, faces the same literacy and numeracy abilities as young people but lags behind colleagues in their 30s and 40s. The Association of Accounting Technicians calls for constant reskilling and retraining to maintain job security in today's rapidly changing economy.

This article highlights two Australian families who have pet kangaroos named Crash and Wally that they take on adventures, feed, bathe, and even bring to social events. Crash, rescued after being thrown from his mother's pouch, and Wally, found abandoned on the side of the road, have become beloved members of their families, bringing joy and companionship to their owners.

A Cleveland police officer, Michael Brelo, is facing voluntary manslaughter charges for shooting and killing unarmed suspects Timothy Russell and Malissa Williams while standing on the hood of their car, during a high-speed chase in 2012. Brelo, the only officer charged out of the 13 who fired their weapons that night, is accused of waiting until after the initial volley of shots to reload and discharge the last of his 49 rounds, which struck the suspects multiple times when they were no longer a threat. Prosecutors argue that Brelo acted out of rage and vengeance, and violated departmental policies, while defense attorneys maintain that he had reason to believe he was being shot at. A judge will decide Brelo's guilt or innocence, and he faces a maximum sentence of 25 years in prison if convicted. Footprints on the hood of the suspects' car matched those found on a police cruiser that Brelo had climbed on top of to fire, and experts have determined that another officer fired three of the shots during Brelo's volley. The incident spurred a US Justice Department probe, revealing a pattern of excessive force by Cleveland police officers, and the city has already paid $3 million to the families of Russell and Williams in a lawsuit settlement.

Izzat Ibrahim al-Douri, a former high-ranking official in Saddam Hussein's regime and a key figure in Sunni extremist groups, including the Naqshbandi Army, which supports ISIS, was reportedly killed in a security operation in Iraq by Iraqi security forces and Shia militia members in the Hamrin Mountains. Al-Douri, the highest-ranking member of Hussein's regime to evade capture, was a military commander and vice chairman of the country's revolutionary command council.

Chelsea midfielder Oscar is attracting interest from Juventus and Liverpool as he faces an uncertain future at Stamford Bridge following criticism from manager Jose Mourinho. The 23-year-old Brazil international, who cost Chelsea £19.35m, has struggled to make an impact this season and Paris Saint-Germain have also shown interest. Juventus are looking for a playmaker and Brazil coach Carlos Dunga has compared Oscar to Roberto Baggio, urging the Italian club to make a move.

A newborn baby in Long Beach, California named Paul was moved to tears by the chorus of the Glee song "O Holy Night" playing on his mother Crystal McNaughton's laptop. The emotional moment, which was captured on film, showed Paul's eyes welling up and his lips quivering, and he repeated the reaction when the song was played again. McNaughton tried to reassure her son that it was just a song, but both she and Paul seemed affected by the emotional music.

The Justice Department filed a brief in a lawsuit against Georgia prison officials for their treatment of transgender inmate Ashley Diamond, who has been denied hormone therapy and safe accommodation, causing severe mental and physical distress, according to the lawsuit. The Department reminded prison officials of their obligation to treat gender dysphoria as they would any other medical or mental health condition under the Eighth Amendment. Diamond, who identified as female since childhood and began hormone therapy at 17, was not evaluated or referred for treatment despite having noticeable feminine physical characteristics and telling staff she was transgender.

Manchester United's Rafael, a Brazilian right-back surplus to requirements at Old Trafford, may have played his last game for the club after sustaining three cracked ribs in a U21 game, effectively ending his season. United are meanwhile looking to boost their squad for the U19 Youth League by scouting young talents, including Luke Tingey and Kyran Wiltshire from MK Dons.

Dundee midfielder Paul McGowan may not play in the remaining six games of the season due to a police curfew imposed after his third assault conviction. Manager Paul Hartley is concerned that even afternoon games could put McGowan at risk of breaching his curfew, and he may only be able to feature in the final-day derby with a special dispensation. Dundee have agreed to sign Aberdeen midfielder Nicky Low on a pre-contract deal.

The heartbroken family of Stephanie Scott, a beloved English teacher who disappeared from Leeton High School and is believed to have been murdered, have shared a sweet photo of her giggling in a bubble bath with her sisters and invited all mourners to show their support at her funeral on Wednesday. The service will be held at the venue intended for her wedding, 'Eat Your Greens' function centre in Eugowra, where her family and friends will remember her life.

Tokyo's Narita Airport's new Terminal 3 features running tracks with directional information and distance markers, intended as a cost-saving measure and an Olympic tribute, leading passengers to departures (blue) and arrivals (red).

The American Pharmacists Association has adopted a ban on its members participating in executions, citing that such activities are contrary to their role as healthcare providers. The association has previously opposed the use of the term "drug" for chemicals used in lethal injections and laws requiring or prohibiting pharmacist involvement. The decision comes amid increased public scrutiny on lethal injection methods and shortages of execution drugs.

Three people, including a security guard and two police officers, were killed and five others were wounded in an ongoing exchange of fire between Afghan security forces and armed assailants who stormed the attorney general's office in Kabul, northern Afghanistan. The security forces are making cautious advances to avoid civilian casualties, according to a press release from the provincial governor's office.

A 16-year-old girl named Isobel Attwood has been missing for two days, and friends believe she is with a man in his 20s in the Southampton area. Isobel posted a message on Facebook apologizing for causing trouble and stating she is safe but needs to get away, leading authorities to express concern for her welfare. Anyone with information is urged to contact the police.

Two deputies involved in the fatal arrest of Eric Harris in Tulsa, Oklahoma, have been reassigned due to threats amid criticism for their role in the incident, during which Reserve Deputy Robert Bates, who is white, accidentally shot and killed Harris, who was black, with a handgun instead of a Taser. The sheriff's office is under scrutiny for allegedly allowing an unqualified deputy to serve and forging training records, and is reviewing its policies and procedures.

The Association of Professional Flight Attendants, representing 25,000 members, has criticized Nicole Kidman for her new ad campaign with Etihad Airways, accusing the airline of discriminatory labor practices and poor treatment of female employees. Etihad denies the allegations and asserts its commitment to employee dignity and respect.

A robotic chef developed by British scientists is set to revolutionize home cooking with its ability to chop, stir, whisk, and baste meals from scratch, using sophisticated robotic arms governed by 26 micro-controllers. The £10,000 ($14,000) device, which includes an oven, hob, dishwasher, and sink, is set to be sold from 2017 and can even order recipes from a professional chef database or learn users' favorite family recipes.

West Ham manager Sam Allardyce has dismissed rumors of offers from Manchester City and Chelsea for left back Aaron Cresswell, urging him to continue his development at West Ham and avoid the potential "disaster" of complacency in his second year in the Premier League. Allardyce emphasized the importance of Cresswell's professionalism and hard work to maintain his top performances.

Australian surfer Josh Kerr suffered a brutal head-first wipe-out while attempting to surf the notorious 'Box' at Margaret River Pro, losing to Hawaiian John John Florence in the competition. Kerr, who finished 2nd in the 2014 event and is currently 6th this year, emerged bruised and beaten from the shallow reef break renowned for its world-class waves and challenging conditions.

A takeaway owner, Kunaratnam Kunanatha, was fined £14,500 and ordered to pay £2,793 in costs after admitting seven food safety and hygiene offenses, including using mouldy tomato puree and selling unfit food, as well as two health and safety charges, at his mice-infested Pizza Plus Fried Chicken business in Gillingham. The premises were immediately closed due to an immediate risk to public health and remained closed permanently after further inspections revealed unsanitary conditions.

A seven-bedroom Tudor mansion in Scarsdale, New York, where real estate heir Robert Durst grew up is on the market for $3.8 million, despite Durst's mother's apparent suicide at the property and his own murder charges in California. The grand and meticulously maintained home features traditional wood paneling, five fireplaces, and a solarium.

A woman identified as 49-year-old Laura Michelle Mayeaux drove the wrong way on Interstate 35E in Texas and collided with oncoming traffic in an apparent suicide attempt, resulting in a multi-vehicle crash that was captured on Jack Rivera's dashcam. Rivera offered assistance at the scene before authorities arrived, and Mayeaux was later found to be intoxicated and underwent treatment at Baylor University Medical Center in Dallas. She admitted to police that she was attempting to take her own life and was released from the hospital last Friday. The incident shut down the highway for around two hours and involved the treatment of two occupants in the pickup truck and the semi-truck. It remains under investigation whether Mayeaux will face charges.

A Northern Territory mother was left shocked and amused after spotting a phallic-shaped cloud during a work break in Zuccoli, 25km southeast of Darwin. She captured the suggestive formation, which she believes someone else must have also seen, and shared it with the media, noting that it's not the first time such a natural phenomenon has occurred.

Convicted murderer Nikko Jenkins, who is facing the possibility of the death penalty for killing four people in Nebraska, attempted to carve the symbol of '666' into his forehead but botched the job, resulting in a series of upside-down 9s. This latest incident, along with his previous self-mutilation of a swastika, may be used as evidence of his mental instability during the upcoming panel to decide his fate, which has been delayed multiple times.

During a Melbourne protest against the 'Islamisation' of Australia, a man with open hands stood up to a swastika-tattooed neo-Nazi who attempted to instigate a fight with anti-racism protesters. The tense atmosphere led to violent clashes between the groups, resulting in injuries and arrests, with police forming a separation wall to maintain order. Australians were urged to remain aware and alert to the potential threats of Islam and sharia law.

Amy Murray, a 23-year-old single mother from Brightlingsea, Essex, was arrested and charged with assault and being drunk and disorderly during a screening of Fifty Shades of Grey after an argument broke out when other moviegoers became annoyed with her laughing at the sex scenes while intoxicated.

Sampdoria are looking to sign Mario Balotelli from Liverpool, with president Massimo Ferrero expressing his belief that the striker has lost his passion for football and needs a change of scenery. Tentative loan offers have been proposed, with Sampdoria potentially offering a cut-price permanent deal, while Balotelli's agent Mino Raiola has hinted at a desire to finish his contract with Liverpool.

ISIS has released disturbing propaganda photos showing off their expanding military markets in Iraq and Syria, where children as young as as young as those in miniature uniforms browse through stalls selling military clothing, tactical vests, holsters, and weapons. The extremist group has converted local markets to supply their growing number of fighters, with items such as knives and camouflage equipment available for purchase. ISIS fighters and local residents are seen freely wandering through the markets, while women are absent. The markets are located in the Iraqi province of Nineveh, where ISIS has been targeting historic sites and carrying out violent campaigns against civilians. The article also mentions the existence of gun stalls and ISIS training camps where new recruits are taught basic skills like disarming enemy riflemen.

England assistant coach Andy Farrell expresses excitement for the upcoming Rugby World Cup in England, noting the buzz around the country and the team's strong showing in the RBS 6 Nations. He also discusses the team's preparations, selection challenges, and the importance of home advantage with matches at Twickenham and Etihad Stadium.

Newcastle United manager John Carver has criticized former player Michael Owen for his pessimistic comments about the team's chances of avoiding relegation, with Owen predicting they would lose every remaining match. Carver, who is dealing with numerous injuries and suspensions, expressed frustration with pundits who have never managed a team. Meanwhile, Newcastle fans are planning a mass protest against owner Mike Ashley due to the club's lack of ambition.

The father of Formula One driver Jules Bianchi, who is still in a coma after sustaining a severe head injury in a crash at the Japanese Grand Prix in 2014, has shared that his condition is stable but his fight for consciousness continues. Bianchi, 25, remains under the watch of neurosurgeons in Nice, and his father described the ongoing anguish and daily torture the family has endured since the accident.

Southampton manager Ronald Koeman is urging his team to focus on their own performances and scoring goals to secure European football, as they have managed just seven goals in their last 10 league matches. Koeman is considering recalling Sadio Mane to the starting lineup to improve their attacking options, as Graziano Pelle continues to struggle, and they face a tough challenge against Hull City, who are fighting to avoid relegation.

A 65-year-old German pensioner and teacher, Annegret Raunigk, is preparing to give birth to quadruplets after her youngest daughter requested a sibling. Raunigk, who already has 13 children, used donated eggs and sperm to conceive due to the advanced age and faces health risks during pregnancy. The decision to have more children has sparked controversy and debate.

A Loose Women panelist, Jamelia, faced backlash on social media after expressing her view that obese women should feel uncomfortable with their unhealthy size and high street stores should not cater to them. The plus-size industry and supporters took to social media to share their opposition to her comments, with the founder of Beauty in Curves denouncing the message as disturbing and exclusionary. Debz, the founder of WeAreTheThey, launched a hashtag campaign to defend plus-size women's right to clothing without feeling uncomfortable. Jamelia later apologized for the misunderstanding and clarified that she was only referring to extreme sizes. Her co-panelists also defended her, but many viewers were critical of her comments.

Green Party leader Natalie Bennett has distanced herself from her boyfriend Jim Jepps, who wrote controversial blog posts defending 'rape fantasies' and describing paedophiles as 'complex human beings.' The Green Party stressed they do not want to be associated with his views, which also questioned why teachers who have relationships with pupils are put on the sex offenders register. Jepps, who met Bennett five years ago, quit the Green Party in 2012, the same year she became leader. He previously wrote about gay sex, music books written by a paedophile, and the case of an Austrian schoolgirl who was kidnapped and held captive for years. Ed Miliband's Labour Party has also faced scrutiny over the private lives of its leaders in contrast to the Green Party, which has seen rising poll ratings and membership.

Morrisons supermarket is introducing halal-only pick and mix counters in 10 stores to cater to Muslim customers, allowing them to sample a selection of 36 sweets without checking the ingredients. Two-year-old Faizaan Ali was among the first to try the new halal sweets in the Blackburn store.

Toby Huntington-Whiteley and Freddie Flintoff, both measuring under 6' 4'' and possessing chiselled looks, model the latest Jacamo summer collection in Majorca. The clothing brand, known for catering to an extensive range of sizes, highlights Toby's supermodel pout and Freddie's energetic personality in their second campaign for the retailer.

The article shares stories and pictures of romantic proposal moments from around the world, as shared on the website and Instagram account HowHeAsked. The proposals include surprise engagements at iconic locations such as the Eiffel Tower, Hawaii's Stairway to Heaven, and Disney World, as well as touching tales of love overcoming adversity.

Gareth Barry, a former Manchester City player and current Everton midfielder, has advised teammate Ross Barkley against joining City at this early stage of his career, citing the Premier League champions as a potential graveyard for young talent. Barry believes Barkley, who has shown great ability but struggled early in the season, is still learning and should focus on improving under Everton's manager.

In a bizarre turn of events during a race in Sydney, Australian jockey Blake Shinn, 27, suffered a wardrobe malfunction, exposing his bum as the elastic in his pants gave way during the final turn. Despite the embarrassing incident, Shinn finished the race with good humor, comparing his situation to that of beauty queen Jennifer Hawkins and jokingly tweeting about it.

A 28-year-old man, Greg Gibbins, and his friend were seriously stabbed during a random attack outside a late-night pizza shop near The Beachcomber Hotel in Toukley, NSW. Gibbins later died in hospital from his injuries, while his friend remains in critical condition. The popular rugby league player was treated at the scene by paramedics before going into cardiac arrest. His grieving family and friends have paid tribute to him on social media.

In the FA Cup semi-final between Reading and Arsenal, Adam Federici made heroic saves to keep the score level at 1-1 but ultimately suffered an embarrassing error in extra time, as Alexis Sanchez's shot squirmed through his legs, allowing Arsenal to progress. Federici, who had been magnificent until then, was inconsolable after the match, with manager Steve Clarke and Arsenal goalkeeper Wojciech Szczesny trying to console him.

Per Mertesacker, Arsenal's central defender, admitted that embarrassing defeats from Manchester City and Liverpool last season led to heated arguments among Arsenal players to regain their competitiveness and improve their performances. This 'arguing culture' has contributed to Arsenal's resurgence this season, as they challenge Manchester City for second place in the Premier League. Arsenal faces Liverpool on Saturday in a crucial match for both teams' Champions League aspirations.

A mother of three, Georgina Rojas-Medina, 41, was shot and killed by her common-law husband, Gerardo Tovar, 44, in Tulare, California, and he then took his own life. The couple's 4-year-old daughter discovered their bodies and left bloody footprints in the aftermath.

Arsenal legend Ray Parlour expresses concerns about Jack Wilshere's injury record and the potential impact it could have on his career, comparing him to Abou Diaby. Wilshere was on the bench during Arsenal's FA Cup final against Reading, and Parlour believes that the midfielder's fitness issues are the only thing holding him back from reaching his full potential.

Eleven channels of the French-language global television network TV5Monde went off air and its social media outlets and websites were temporarily taken over by an Islamist group in a powerful cyberattack, leaving over 260 million homes worldwide without access to the network's entertainment, news, and cultural programming. The identity of the group responsible has not been confirmed.

An expensive New York City apartment building, The Osborne on West 57th Street, is reportedly haunted by the friendly ghosts of opera singer Johanna Gadski and architect Alfredo Taylor. Actress Jessica Chastain, who recently purchased a $5.1 million apartment in the building, joins a long list of residents who have experienced paranormal activity, including doors opening on their own and random toilet flushing.

Chinese designers have created a new invention called the Bike Washing Machine, which combines a washing machine and exercise bike, allowing users to pedal to clean their clothes and generate electricity for future use. The device, currently in prototype stage, could help those struggling to find space for both appliances and reduce electricity bills. Users would need to add detergent and water before each cycle.

Two identical twins, Marcus and Markieff Morris, both forwards for the Phoenix Suns in the NBA, are under investigation for allegedly assaulting their former mentor, Erik Hood, at a sports complex in January after he sent "inappropriate" text messages to their mother. No charges have been filed, but Hood was hospitalized with injuries.

The Dawn spacecraft has captured new images of the mysterious bright spots on Ceres, a dwarf planet in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter, from a distance of 14,000 miles. Experts are still unsure of their composition, with theories including salt flats, ice, cryovolcanoes, or water vapor. Dawn, which became the first spacecraft to orbit a dwarf planet in March 2015, will continue to approach Ceres and begin its first mapping phase on April 23. The spacecraft left the asteroid Vesta in 2012 and is seen as a record of the early solar system. Both Vesta and Ceres were on their way to forming planets before their development was interrupted by Jupiter's gravity.

A large-scale brawl involving at least five women broke out at a Zara clothing store in Philadelphia's Rittenhouse Square neighborhood, with the women pulling hair, slapping, yanking clothes, and knocking over displays. The police were called but all of the women had fled by the time they arrived, and no arrests were made. The cause of the fight remains unclear.

In a thrilling Scottish Cup semi-final, David Raven scored a last-gasp winner for Inverness CT to secure their first-ever William Hill Scottish Cup final appearance against Falkirk, leaving Celtic devastated after a topsy-turvy match that saw both teams have a player sent off. An emotional Raven celebrated with his teammates after the dramatic 3-2 victory.

The article discusses various topics that have been making people feel warm or cold this week, including Australia's triumph in the Cricket World Cup, Colin Graves' plans to save Test cricket, Tracey Neville's appointment as England netball coach, Kevin Sinfield's switch from rugby league to union, Roberto Mancini's struggling Inter Milan team, Rickie Lambert's struggles at Liverpool, and Phil Jones' performance at Manchester United. Additionally, Butch Harmon's suggestion for Rickie Fowler to become "nasty" to win the US Masters is also mentioned.

Andy Lee, the Irish WBO middleweight champion, is confident of a successful first defence against Peter Quillin in New York, having made significant improvements since winning the title last year. Both fighters express self-assurance and expect a technical yet explosive bout, with Quillin focusing on overcoming his own struggles.

A fearless lion and its companions attempted to take down a hippo calf at Kruger National Park in South Africa, but the mother hippo intervened and the lions left empty-handed. Incredible images captured the moment the lion bit the calf's back, but the hippo escaped unscathed.

In a surprise turn of events, Nicola Sturgeon, the Scottish National Party leader, was declared the shock winner of the TV debate according to one poll, dealing a damaging blow to Ed Miliband's election hopes as Labour battles to fend off an SNP landslide in Scotland. Sturgeon was deemed to have outperformed Miliband, with the Conservatives also emerging with a clear lead on who is seen as most capable of leading the country.

In the FA Youth Cup final first leg, Chelsea's Tammy Abraham and Dominic Solanke continued their impressive goalscoring form with a combined total of 37 goals, helping Chelsea establish a commanding 3-1 lead over Manchester City. Chelsea look poised to retain the Youth Cup title as they dominate possession and press hard, with Abraham's unstoppable right foot providing two of the goals. The second leg will take place at Stamford Bridge in seven days' time.

Wigan Warriors forward Ben Flower expressed deep regret over his brutal attack on Lance Hohaia during the Super League Grand Final, which went viral and led to a six-month ban. He is now preparing to return to the game and make amends, promising to come back as a changed man and make a positive impact on the pitch against Warrington.

Sporty Pippa Middleton was spotted jogging in London for the second time this week, preparing for a 54-mile charity bike ride for the British Heart Foundation. She was dressed in Adidas climachill shorts and coordinating top, showcasing her toned physique.

In this heartwarming yet tragic story, Ben and Shelby Offrink, who met in a calculus-based physics study group and eventually married with two young children, face unexpected challenges as Shelby is diagnosed with a rare and aggressive form of brain cancer, and Ben's Hodgkins lymphoma returns, leaving them both undergoing chemotherapy while trying to raise their family. Despite the challenges, their strong spirit and determination to overcome their illnesses keep them fighting.

During a campaign visit to Devon, Prime Minister David Cameron made a blunder by confusing the order for adding jam and cream to a scone, stating the Devon tradition is jam first and cream on top, but was corrected and admitted he got it wrong. The jam and cream debate, which divides the Westcountry, has been ongoing for generations with references dating back to the 11th century, and an academic's mathematical formula for the perfect cream tea supports the Cornish way of spreading jam first.

Don McLean's manuscript of the iconic song "American Pie," which sold for $1.2 million at auction, is the third highest price ever paid for an American literary manuscript. McLean, who turns 70 this year, decided it was time to sell the 16-page draft, which includes lines that didn't make the final version and extensive notes. Despite its length and complex symbolism, "American Pie" catapulted McLean to headliner status and reached No. 1 in 1972.

A new book by former White House correspondent Kate Andersen Brower claims that Hillary Clinton imposed a strict secrecy on her movements during the Monica Lewinsky scandal in 1998, instructing aides and the Secret Service that she could only be seen by one usher when going to the pool, and had the White House telephone system completely rewired for privacy. This new revelation could harm Clinton's chances in the 2016 presidential race, as a poll shows her approval ratings have slipped since the scandal and she is now two points behind former Florida governor Jeb Bush.

Cricket commentator and former Australia captain Richie Benaud, aged 84, passed away peacefully in his sleep after a battle with skin cancer. Prime Minister Tony Abbott offered his family a state funeral in recognition of Benaud's towering stature in cricket, with tributes pouring in for the much-admired and influential figure. As a player, Benaud was a trailblazing all-rounder and captain, and later became a broadcasting icon known for his dry wit and expertise. Benaud had a long career with the BBC and Nine Network, establishing one-day cricket on television and leaving a lasting impact on the sport in Australia and beyond. He is survived by his wife Daphne.

A lawsuit filed by a grandmother alleges SeaWorld is keeping orcas in shallow pools, causing severe sunburns that require staff to paint them with zinc oxide to hide the injuries. Joyce Kuhl accuses the company of false business practices, depriving animals of food during training, and maintaining inadequate enclosures. She seeks ticket refunds for herself and millions of visitors. Similar allegations have been made in a California lawsuit.

Two base jumpers, Austin Carey and Jay Rawe, who suffered broken backs after their parachutes tangled during a jump at Idaho's Perrine Bridge, have miraculously made full recoveries and returned to the sport they love, with Carey even successfully jumping from the bridge again.

Ed Miliband faced severe criticism for suggesting that David Cameron bears personal responsibility for the drownings of refugees in the Mediterranean, with some accusing him of making political capital from the tragedy and even "weaponising dead migrants." The Labour leader claimed the Mediterranean crisis was a result of the PM's Libya policy, but critics argued it was an ill-judged and negative attempt to score points. Both the Liberal Democrats and senior Conservatives condemned the intervention, accusing Labour of hypocrisy and reducing the human tragedy to a political blame game.

Rafa Benitez is known for his cup successes in European football, most recently leading Napoli to a dominating victory over Wolfsburg in the Europa League. However, despite managing prominent clubs like Liverpool, Inter, Chelsea, and Napoli, he has yet to win a league title. Benitez's tenure at Liverpool, marked by cup triumphs but inconsistent league performance, left a mixed legacy among fans.

Fernando Alonso, a two-time Formula One world champion, announced that he will retire from the sport once his contract with McLaren-Honda expires, stating that he has reached a maturity where winning trophies is no longer his priority. Alonso, who has spent one third of his life with McLaren-Honda, expressed satisfaction with his current team experience despite their struggles with pace and reliability.

The execution of Australian citizens Andrew Chan and Myuran Sukumaran in Indonesia will lead to short-term diplomatic frictions between Canberra and Jakarta, but their deaths will have longer-lasting effects on Australia's view of Indonesia. The executions came despite international appeals for clemency, and the lack of communication from Indonesian authorities fueled growing anger and mistrust in Australia. Prior to Chan and Sukumaran, the last Australian executed abroad for drug offenses was Van Tuong Nguyen in Singapore in 2005.

PSG's Brazilian player Lucas Moura expressed his admiration for Argentine football icon Lionel Messi, despite the historical rivalry between their countries. Moura, who idolizes Messi and is "obsessed" with his playing style, acknowledged the challenge of facing him in their upcoming Champions League match against Barcelona.

The article discusses the Manchester Derby between Manchester United and Manchester City, with United securing a 4-2 victory and taking a four-point lead over their rivals. The piece questions whether this win signifies a return to dominance for United or just a bad game for City, who have struggled this season and currently sit in third place. The article also touches upon Manchester United's improvement under Louis van Gaal and the challenges facing Manchester City's Manuel Pellegrini, who could be under pressure to keep his job.

A 7.8-magnitude earthquake triggered an avalanche on Mount Everest, crushing tents and killing 18 people, including four Americans. Survivors described being dragged by the wind and snow, and digging their way out of the debris. Before and after images show the devastating destruction of the base camp, with videos and testimonies adding to the chaos and desperation. Three Americans killed were Google executive Dan Fredinburg, medic Dr Marisa Eve Girawong, and documentary filmmaker Tom Taplin, who owned TET Films. Rescuers are working to recover survivors and the dead, as the number of casualties is expected to rise.

A video shows a strong-willed toddler named Catalina from America refusing to clean up her mess in the kitchen, leading to a heated argument with her father. Despite her father's insistence, Catalina refuses to pick up the kitchen implements and instead engages in a back-and-forth standoff with him. The video ends with Catalina being left alone in the kitchen.

Countryfile presenter Ellie Harrison, 37, accepts that she will eventually be replaced on the BBC show due to the nature of the industry and the perceived expectations for female presenters, having previously been asked to tone down her appearance. She has spoken about the challenges of being judged for her looks and age in the industry, following in the footsteps of Miriam O'Reilly who successfully sued the BBC for age discrimination after being dropped from the show in 2009.
